old_claim.note: Fine Arts (Medieval) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Late medieval art supplied the immediate artistic background from which Renaissance perspective emerged, while true linear perspective was formalized later by Brunelleschi and Alberti.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica notes Giotto's early treatment of painting as a window into space and says true linear perspective was later formalized by Brunelleschi and Alberti.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source puts Renaissance perspective in a sequence from late medieval spatial representation to formalized linear perspective.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The edge is retyped to historical_predecessor because medieval fine arts supply prior artistic lineage, not an enabling technical component.

ontology_before: Perspective in Art treated Romanesque and Gothic Fine Arts as an enabling capability for Renaissance perspective.
ontology_after: Perspective in Art treats medieval fine arts as predecessor artistic lineage while geometry and mathematics remain technical enablers.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: fine_arts_medieval is now a historical_predecessor edge.
would_reject_if: The node is broadened to all medieval spatial representation.
A more specific proto_perspective_painting node is added and should replace fine_arts_medieval.
A stronger source shows medieval fine arts directly supplied a reusable technical method equivalent to linear perspective.
short_reason: Medieval fine arts are lineage, not a direct enabling method.
